explanation_md: The second `std::cout` line is inside a `//` comment, so the compiler ignores it entirely. Only `"Start\n"` and `"End\n"` are executed, printing two lines.

prompt_md: What happens when the compiler encounters a comment?

options.a: It stores the comment text in the compiled binary for later inspection.
options.b: It skips the comment entirely; comments have no effect on the program.
options.c: It prints the comment text when the program runs.
options.d: It raises a warning if the comment is longer than 80 characters.

explanation_md: Comments are stripped out during compilation. They leave no trace in the compiled binary and produce no output at runtime. They exist solely for human readers of the source code.

options.a: `std::cout` cannot appear inside a multi-line comment.
options.b: The inner `*/` on line 7 closes the outer `/*` comment, leaving `std::cout << "World\n";` and the stray `*/` outside the comment, causing a syntax error.
options.c: Multi-line comments must start on their own line, not after code.
options.d: `#include <iostream>` must appear inside the comment block.

explanation_md: Multi-line `/* ... */` comments do not nest. The `*/` that ends the inner comment also ends the outer one. Everything after it — including `std::cout << "World\n";` and the orphaned `*/` — is treated as code, producing a compile error. Use `//` when commenting out code that already contains `/* ... */`.

prompt_md: Which comment style is safest when you want to temporarily disable a single statement?

options.a: `/* */` because it can span multiple lines.
options.b: `//` because it ends automatically at the line boundary and cannot accidentally swallow adjacent code.
options.c: Both are equally safe for a single statement.
options.d: Neither; you should delete the statement instead.

explanation_md: `//` is safer for disabling a single statement because its scope is exactly one line — it cannot accidentally disable adjacent lines. `/* */` requires you to place the closing `*/` correctly, which is error-prone when the block grows or contains inner comments.
